channel.title: Nika · Notes from the source
channel.description: Long-form pedagogy on Intent as Code: why useful AI work belongs in a file, why the language locks at four verbs, and what local-first actually buys you.

item.title: Written by agents, reviewed by you
item.description: nika init teaches the language to whatever agent you already run. Then two real agents write the same workflow: one invents a schema the audit refuses, one goes green first shot — and the human review is a two-line diff.

item.title: The run that waits for you
item.description: The approval gate is a task in the file, not a Slack thread: a plan fails closed on nika:prompt, and the human's answer rides the resume.

item.title: The resume story
item.description: kill -9 a run mid-flight, then resume it: finished work never runs twice. Durability as a file property, shown from the real journal.

item.title: The one-task re-run
item.description: Regenerate one block without re-running the world: --task scopes a fresh run to a task and its upstream, --from re-rolls what the hashes cannot see.

item.title: Prompts are code now
item.description: Prompt versioning without a platform: the prompt lives in the workflow file, so git diffs it, a PR reviews it, git reverts it — and the engine itself names an edited prompt when two runs diverge.

item.title: The prompt injection that goes nowhere
item.description: Prompt injection examples usually end with the agent taking a new action. Here the plan is authored before the model runs — the hostile note becomes data, never an action, and the boundary is checked before a token is spent.

item.title: The run becomes evidence
item.description: Every journal line now carries a hash chain — verify names the first broken link, reproduce classifies every task, and the journal attests which engine wrote it. Trust, but verify. Then verify.

item.title: Time travel, for real
item.description: Every debugger replays the past by re-running it. Nika's past is a file — so stepping backward is free, breakpoints live in your YAML, and F5 debugs a run that already happened.

item.title: The editor tells the truth
item.description: A green badge is a claim. We audited the editor extension against the engine the way you'd audit a client against a server — and found the badge lying. Here is the contract that keeps it honest now.

item.title: The credentials your pipeline was breaking
item.description: OpenAI and Google sign the images their APIs return. Almost every pipeline that touches those files silently converts the signature into evidence of tampering — including, until this week, ours.

item.title: One wire, five servers
item.description: The self-hosted media world quietly standardized on OpenAI's wire shapes. That accident of history is the best sovereignty news in years — and Nika's media builtins are built on it.

item.title: Media are workflow citizens
item.description: Images and speech now render inside workflows under the same discipline as everything else: permit-gated saves, sha256 provenance, honest warnings, real cost on the ledger — and the sovereign path first.

item.title: The trace you can replay
item.description: Every run leaves a flight recorder: a deterministic AI audit trail you can replay like a film, never re-execute by accident.

item.title: The secrets line
item.description: Information-flow, audited before it flows: how the checker proves a secret cannot leak into a prompt, a file, or a host.

item.title: The cost line
item.description: The audit prices every token before it is spent: ceilings on generations, budgets on loops, and a $0.00 that means it.

item.title: Anatomy of a verb
item.description: infer, exec, invoke, agent: what makes the four verbs genuinely different execution models, in the engine's own verdicts.

item.title: No cloud needed
item.description: One Rust binary, your models, your files. Run LLMs locally and see what local-first actually buys you.

item.title: The plan you get for free
item.description: depends_on is all you write. The orchestration falls out of the graph: parallel waves, drawn before anything runs.

item.title: Four verbs are enough
item.description: A verb is a distinct execution model, not a feature. Why the language locks at four, forever.

item.title: Intent as Code: why your AI work should be a file
item.description: Chats evaporate, files compound. The case for writing AI work down as source you own: your best prompts, versioned like code.

item.title: The blast radius is part of the file
item.description: permits: is the whole list, guardrails an agent can't talk past. Everything not on it is denied before it runs, with a named error.

item.title: A standard library, not a plugin store
item.description: 25 builtins in the binary, allow-listed, nothing to install. The library grows, the language holds still.

item.title: An open spec, a copyleft engine
item.description: Two licenses, one argument: the file must outlive every vendor, including us.

item.title: Starting over, on purpose
item.description: We had a working prototype. We rebuilt from scratch anyway: craft, not extraction, one gate at a time.

item.title: Naming the drum
item.description: Why a workflow engine carries the name of a liberation god, and a butterfly on every commit.

item.title: The note that started it
item.description: Before the spec, before the name, there was one evening note: the best AI work of the month had just disappeared.
